did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used DeepLabv3+ for semantic segmentation of skin lesions and a multi-scale classification system (utilizing ResNet50V2) to classify skin lesions into three classes (seborrheic keratosis, melanoma, and nevus) using the ISIC 2017 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design and assessment of an automated skin lesion classification model using DeepLabv3+ and ResNet50V2 on the ISIC 2017 dataset. It includes a methodology section, dataset description, training details (30 epochs, Adam optimizer), and quantitative results (accuracy, AUC, confusion matrices) comparing segmented vs. non-segmented approaches. Although published in a conference (IEEE INDICON), it reports new empirical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Effect of Segmentation on Skin Lesion Classification Using Multi-Scale Convolution Neural Networks
brief_summary: This study investigates the impact of region of interest (ROI) extraction on the classification of skin lesions into seborrheic keratosis, melanoma, and nevus using the ISIC 2017 dataset. The proposed methodology employs DeepLabv3+ for semantic segmentation followed by a multi-scale classification system utilizing ResNet50V2. The authors compared the performance of models trained on segmented images against those trained on non-segmented images to assess the value of preprocessing. Quantitative evaluation revealed that the segmentation-based approach achieved an overall accuracy of 83%, representing a 4% improvement over the 79% accuracy of the non-segmented baseline. The results demonstrate that emphasizing the lesion region reduces reliance on background contextual information, leading to more robust and dependable classification predictions.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three primary classes in the ISIC 2017 dataset used for multi-class classification. The study reports specific counts (90 initial, balanced to 393 via augmentation) and performance metrics (Precision 0.81, Recall 0.76, F1 0.78 for segmented model).

One of the three primary classes in the ISIC 2017 dataset used for multi-class classification. The study reports specific counts (117 initial, balanced to 393 via augmentation) and performance metrics (Precision 0.80, Recall 0.85, F1 0.82 for segmented model). Also used in binary classification benchmarks against seborrheic keratosis.

One of the three primary classes in the ISIC 2017 dataset used for multi-class classification. The study reports specific counts (393 initial, balanced to 393 via augmentation) and performance metrics (Precision 0.87, Recall 0.87, F1 0.87 for segmented model).

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (seborrheic keratosis, melanoma, and nevus)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions into three distinct categories (seborrheic keratosis, melanoma, and nevus) using the ISIC 2017 dataset. It also reports binary classification benchmarks (melanoma vs. seborrheic keratosis). The model outputs probabilities for each class via a softmax activation layer to determine the most likely class.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion region of interest (ROI) mask
all_task_types.details: The paper utilizes DeepLabv3+ for semantic segmentation to extract the region of interest (ROI) from dermoscopic images. This segmentation is used as a preprocessing step for the classification task, not as the primary diagnostic output.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration containing 2,750 dermoscopic images used for skin lesion classification. The study utilized a modified train-validation split of 80:20, with 600 test images reserved for final assessment.

use.training: Used for training the multi-scale classification model (ResNet50V2) after augmentation and splitting into training, validation, and testing sets.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process to monitor performance and implement early stopping/learning rate reduction.
use.testing: Used for final assessment and evaluation of the model's performance (accuracy, AUC, confusion matrices).
use.other: Used for semantic segmentation training with DeepLabv3+ to extract regions of interest.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristics Curve (AUC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To overcome class imbalance in the dataset and enhance the model’s generalization capabilities by applying transformations such as vertical flipping, horizontal mirroring, random brightness adjustments, random contrast modifications, and random rotations.
To optimize training and prevent overfitting by monitoring validation performance.
To prevent overfitting in the classification model.
To stabilize and accelerate the training process.
To convert the probability map to a binary mask during post-processing of segmentation results.
To widen the segmented region, close gaps, and seal edges in the binary mask.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between segmented and non-segmented models were based on descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and AUC.

ISIC 2017.notes: The segmented model achieved an overall accuracy of 83%, a 4% improvement over the non-segmented baseline (79%). The segmentation model (DeepLabv3+) achieved 89% accuracy. Binary classification benchmarks showed the proposed models outperformed or matched Matsunaga et al. in AUC and sensitivity for melanoma.

architecture_details.segmentation_features: Encoder-decoder structure with spatial pyramid pooling, atrous (dilated) convolutions, and Squeeze and Excitation (SE) blocks
architecture_details.classification_features: Multi-scale approach with pooled feature vectors from each scale concatenated into a single comprehensive vector

classification_or_output_head.segmentation_output: Binary mask generated via Otsu’s Adaptive Thresholding on probability map, followed by morphological processing (dilation and closure)
classification_or_output_head.classification_output: Softmax activation layer for multi-class probabilities (seborrheic keratosis, melanoma, nevus)

preprocessing_techniques: Shades of Gray color constancy algorithm for balancing color distribution and reducing inter-image variability
Data augmentation including vertical flipping, horizontal mirroring, random brightness adjustments, random contrast modifications, and random rotations to handle class imbalance
Image resizing to multiple scales (224x224, 336x336, and 448x448 pixels) for multi-scale feature extraction
Global average pooling to summarize features into a single vector per scale
ROI extraction via bitwise AND operation using post-processed binary segmentation masks to isolate the lesion region
Post-processing of segmentation masks using Otsu’s adaptive thresholding to convert probability maps to binary masks
Morphological processing (dilation and closure) to widen segmented regions, close gaps, and seal edges

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied stochastically to images to handle class imbalance and enhance generalization.
Applied stochastically to images to handle class imbalance and enhance generalization.
Applied stochastically to images to handle class imbalance and enhance generalization.
Applied stochastically to images to handle class imbalance and enhance generalization.

Used to overcome class imbalance; increased counts for seborrheic keratosis (from 90) and melanoma (from 117) to match nevus (393), resulting in 393 images per class.

conclusions: A two-stage process involving segmentation followed by classification is more effective than a straight classification strategy for skin lesion analysis.
The segmentation-based approach achieved an overall accuracy of 83%, representing a 4% improvement over the 79% accuracy of the non-segmented baseline.
Emphasizing the lesion region reduces reliance on background contextual information, leading to more robust and dependable classification predictions.
Non-segmented models may offer higher accuracy due to increased contextual information, but their predictability may be less reliable compared to segmented approaches.
The proposed multi-scale classification system effectively addresses the issue of background reliance by using different resolutions, resulting in a robust and dependable classification system.
important_remarks: The study intentionally used a single dataset (ISIC 2017) and avoided complex ensemble strategies to keep the focus on the methodological comparison of segmentation versus non-segmentation, limiting generalizability to other datasets or populations.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, AUC, etc.).

The dataset was heavily augmented to balance classes (increasing seborrheic keratosis from 90 and melanoma from 117 to 393 to match nevus), which may introduce bias or overfitting risks not fully addressed.
The paper does not report external validation; results are confined to the modified 80:20 split of the ISIC 2017 dataset.

While the segmented model showed a 4% accuracy improvement (83% vs 79%), the binary classification benchmark showed only marginal differences in AUC (0.92 vs 0.93) compared to the non-segmented model, suggesting the benefit of segmentation might be context-dependent.

The authors acknowledge that non-segmented models may offer higher accuracy due to increased contextual information but argue their predictability is less reliable; however, this claim is supported primarily by Grad-CAM visualizations rather than quantitative robustness metrics.
Deployment concerns are not addressed; the study focuses on offline classification performance rather than real-time inference speed, computational cost, or clinical integration workflows.
